coredata.dc:title: A simple fluorescent “on-off-on” nanosensor based on nitrogen-doped carbon dots for selective detection of Hg2+ and thiamine
coredata.dc:creator: Thanomsak, Suphakan
Kerdphon, Sutthichat
Sirikulkajorn, Anchalee
Tuntulani, Thawatchai
Janrungroatsakul, Wanwisa
coredata.dc:description: A fluorescent “on-off-on” nanosensor based on nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) was developed for the detection of Hg2+ and thiamine (vitamin B1). N-CDs were synthesized through a one-step microwave heating approach using d-fructose and l-lysine as precursors in aqueous media. The as-synthesized N-CDs exhibited a strong fluorescent emission peak at 440 nm when excited at 360 nm. The fluorescence emission intensity of synthesized N-CDs was quenched when adding Hg2+ (turn off) at concentrations between 1 and 10 μM, with limit of detection (LOD) 0.40 μM due to the formation of a complex between N-CDs and Hg2+. By contrast, quenched fluorescence of the N-CD/Hg2+ system was recovered by adding thiamine that effectively removed Hg2+ from the N-CD surface resulting in fluorescence turning on. The intensity of the restored fluorescence was proportional to the concentration of thiamine, ranging from 0.2 to 2.0 μM with LOD down to 30 nM. The “on-off-on” process of N-CD fluorescence probes was successfully applied to determine Hg2+ in real water and cosmetic samples and thiamine in grain food samples with accurate results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Fluorescent sensor
Mercury(II) ion detection
Thiamine detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence determination of Hg2+ and thiamine
Sample preparation for determination of Hg2+ and thiamine in real samples
Analytical performance of N-CDs for Hg2+
Detection of Hg2+ in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: School of Aerospace Science and Technology
Department of Chemistry, University of York

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was financially supported by the Faculty of Science, Naresuan University (grant number R2565E033). W. J. acknowledges financial and laboratory support from the Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Naresuan University. S. T. was supported by the Science Achievement Scholarship of Thailand (SAST).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image, (b) size distribution, (c) HR-TEM image of synthesized N-CDs, (d) XPS survey spectrum of N-CDs and high-resolution spectra for (e) C 1s, (f) O 1s, and (g) N 1s.

(a) UV–vis absorption, fluorescence excitation, and emission spectra of N-CDs. The insets are photographs of N-CDs under visible light and 365 nm UV light, (b) fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs excited with different wavelengths ranging from 300 to 400 nm, and (c) photoluminescence excitation 2D plot of N-CDs.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of N-CDs, N-CDs/Hg2+ (10 μM) and N-CDs/Hg2+ (10 μM)/thiamine (2 μM) in 1 M acetic-acetate buffer pH 6, (b) time-resolved fluorescence decay curves of N-CDs in the absence and presence of Hg2+ (10 μM), (c) fluorescence spectra of N-CDs after addition of various concentrations of Hg2+, and (d) Stern-Volmer plot of fluorescence quenching of N-CDs by Hg2+.
(a) Fluorescence spectra of N-CD solutions on addition of various metal cations (6 μM), and (b) the corresponding degree of fluorescence quenching in the presence of various metal cations.

Linear relationship between F
0 –F/F
0 and Hg2+ concentration ranging from 1 to 10 μM.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of N-CDs/Hg2+ (10 μM) in the presence of 1 μM of various substances, (b) corresponding degree of fluorescence restored in the presence of various substances. F and F
r are the fluorescence intensities of N-CDs/Hg2+ (10 μM) in the absence and presence of various substances, (c) fluorescence spectra of N-CDs/Hg2+ with addition of different concentrations of thiamine (0–3 μM), and (d) linear plot of fluorescence recovery efficiency (F r–F)/F versus thiamine concentration ranging from 0.2 to 2.0 μM.
Reversibility of N-CD fluorescence by alternative addition of Hg2+ and thiamine in acetic-acetate buffer.
Determination of Hg2+ in water and whitening cream samples (n = 3).
Determination of thiamine in real grain food samples (n = 3).
Comparison of the proposed method with reported methods for detecting thiamine.

head.ce:title: A simple fluorescent “on-off-on” nanosensor based on nitrogen-doped carbon dots for selective detection of Hg2+ and thiamine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Naresuan University, Phitsanulok, 65000, Thailand
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Naresuan University, Phitsanulok 65000, Thailand

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Chulalongkorn University, Bangkok, 10330, Thailand
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Chulalongkorn University, Bangkok 10330, Thailand

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Naresuan University, Phitsanulok, 65000, Thailand.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A fluorescent “on-off-on” nanosensor based on nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) was developed for the detection of Hg2+ and thiamine (vitamin B1). N-CDs were synthesized through a one-step microwave heating approach using d-fructose and l-lysine as precursors in aqueous media. The as-synthesized N-CDs exhibited a strong fluorescent emission peak at 440 nm when excited at 360 nm. The fluorescence emission intensity of synthesized N-CDs was quenched when adding Hg2+ (turn off) at concentrations between 1 and 10 μM, with limit of detection (LOD) 0.40 μM due to the formation of a complex between N-CDs and Hg2+. By contrast, quenched fluorescence of the N-CD/Hg2+ system was recovered by adding thiamine that effectively removed Hg2+ from the N-CD surface resulting in fluorescence turning on. The intensity of the restored fluorescence was proportional to the concentration of thiamine, ranging from 0.2 to 2.0 μM with LOD down to 30 nM. The “on-off-on” process of N-CD fluorescence probes was successfully applied to determine Hg2+ in real water and cosmetic samples and thiamine in grain food samples with accurate results.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are the rising stars of fluorescence nanomaterials due to their simple preparation, excellent optical properties, high photostability, good biocompatibility and low toxicity [1,2]. CDs have attracted increasing interest in bioimaging applications, drug delivery, photocatalysis, light-emitting diodes, and fluorescent detection [3–8]. Synthetic development methods of CDs include discharge, laser ablation, electrochemical oxidation, and hydrothermal and microwave pyrolysis of different carbonaceous sources [5,9]. CDs doped with heteroatoms [10–13] such as nitrogen, sulfur, boron, and phosphorous show interesting chemical and physical properties. For heteroatoms, nitrogen doping is most widely used since the atomic size of nitrogen is close to that of carbon. Nitrogen atoms containing five valence electrons bind with carbon atoms and this effectively changes the electronic structure resulting in improved fluorescence properties [14].

CDs are often used as fluorescent probes for direct quenching detection which is also termed fluorescence “turn off”. Recently, CDs have been developed as “on-off-on” fluorescent probes. The fluorescence of CDs is quenched by the first analyte and then recovered by the addition of a second analyte which has stronger interactions [8]. Jia et al. investigated CD fluorescent probes for “on-off-on” detection of Hg2+ and biothiols (cysteine, homocysteine, and glutathione) [15]. The fluorescence response of CDs was quenched by Hg2+, while the fluorescence quenching of the CD/Hg2+ system was recovered in the presence of biothiols. The fluorescence recovery of CDs was attributed to the separation of Hg2+ by biothiols because the interaction between biothiols and Hg2+ was stronger than the binding affinity between CDs and Hg2+. The thiol group in the biothiols interacted with Hg2+ to form a more stable Hg–S bond, and then Hg2+ was released from the CD surface resulting in fluorescence turning on.

Thiamine (better well-known as vitamin B1) is a sulfur-containing water-soluble vitamin. This essential nutrient acts as a coenzyme in the metabolism that converts carbohydrates, amino acids, and lipids into energy sources for the human body [16]. The recommended intake of thiamine suggested by the World Health Organization (WHO) is 1.1 and 1.2 mg/day for women and men, respectively [17]. For pregnant women, thiamine consumption is recommended at 1.4 mg/day to prevent risks in the central nervous development of babies in the womb. Insufficiency of thiamine can lead to health problems, especially related to the peripheral nervous and cardiovascular systems [18]. Lack of thiamine causes Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome and beriberi disease [19]. Thiamine cannot be synthesized by the human body and must be obtained externally through a variety of foodstuffs such as rice, oats, whole grains, legumes, nuts, yeast, beef, pork, eggs, fruits, and vegetables.

This study focused on the development of an “on-off-on” sensor based on N-CDs as a fluorescence probe for the determination of Hg2+ and thiamine. First, N-CDs were prepared by microwave pyrolysis using d-fructose and l-lysine as precursors. The synthesized N-CDs showed a selective and sensitive fluorescence quenching response toward Hg2+ (turn off), with the quenched fluorescence of the N-CD/Hg2+ system recovered in the presence of thiamine (turn on). N-CDs can be used as an “on-off-on” fluorescent sensor for the quantitative detection of Hg2+ and thiamine in real water and food samples, respectively.

All chemical reagents were of analytical grade and they were used directly without any further purification. d-Fructose was provided by KemAus. l-Lysine monohydrochloride, sodium acetate anhydrous and thiamine hydrochloride (vitamin B1) were sourced from Sigma-Aldrich. Glacial acetic acid and mercury(II) chloride were purchased from Merck. Nitric acid was obtained from RCI Labscan. Ultrapure water with a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ cm (ELGA, High Wycombe, Bucks, UK) was used in all experiments.

Fluorescence spectra were recorded with a JASCO FP-8500 Spectrometer (JASCO Corporation, Osaka, Japan). UV–visible spectra were measured using a double beam Specord 200 plus Spectrophotometer (Analytik Jena AG, Jena, Germany). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were collected on an IRTracer-100 Spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Corporation, Tokyo, Japan). Confocal Raman spectroscopy was performed on a LabRAM HR Evolution (Horiba Corporation, Kyoto, Japan). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were collected using a Thermo Fisher Talos F200X (JEOL Corporation, Japan). Dynamic light scattering (DLS) data were obtained using a Zetasizer Nano ZS, (Malvern Instruments, UK). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were performed using a Kratos Axis Ultra DLD Spectrometer (Shimadzu Corporation, Tokyo, Japan). X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements were examined by an Expert Diffractometer (Malvern Instruments, UK). The pH values of the solutions were measured by a Mettler-Toledo Sevencompact duo S213–K (Columbus, Ohio, USA).

The N-CDs were synthesized following the microwave-assisted method using d-fructose and l-lysine as precursors. First, 1 g of d-fructose and 2 mg of l-lysine were mixed with 10 mL of ultrapure water in a 50 mL beaker under vigorous stirring until the solid was completely dissolved. A watch glass was placed over the beaker containing the mixture solution, which was then placed in a domestic microwave oven and irradiated at 700 W for 10 min. The dark brown viscous liquid obtained was allowed to cool naturally to room temperature. The ultrapure water (10 mL) was added to the beaker, followed by stirring the solution with a stirring rod before purifying by filtration through a 0.22 μm nylon filter membrane to remove the large-sized particles. The obtained N-CD solution was stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C before use. The concentration of N-CDs was 0.55 M, calculated from the concentration of d-fructose.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence determination of Hg2+ and thiamine
ce:section.ce:para: Detection of Hg2+ was performed at room temperature in aqueous solution. First, the N-CDs were diluted in ultrapure water to prepare a stock solution of 0.01 M N-CDs. In a typical experiment, 800 μL of the 0.01 M N-CD solution, 1000 μL of 1 M acetic-acetate buffer pH 6, and an appropriate volume of a standard solution of Hg2+ were added to a 10 mL volumetric flask, and the solution was then adjusted to a final volume of 10 mL with ultrapure water. The mixed solution was incubated for 10 min. Finally, the fluorescence intensity was monitored at 440 nm with an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. All measurements were carried out in triplicate.

For thiamine determination, 800 μL of 0.01 M N-CD solution, 1000 μL of 1 M acetic-acetate buffer pH 6, and 1000 μL of 0.1 mM Hg2+ were added to a 10 mL volumetric flask. The mixed solution was adjusted with ultrapure water to a final volume of 10 mL before incubating for 10 min at room temperature. A known volume of 0.1 mM thiamine was then added to the solution, with a further incubation for 5 min. The fluorescence spectrum was recorded under an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. Each thiamine concentration measurement was performed in triplicate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sample preparation for determination of Hg2+ and thiamine in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence sensing of N-CDs to determine the amount of Hg2+ was performed for both water and whitening cream samples. Environmental water samples obtained from various sources (drinking water, pond water, and tap water) were filtered through Whatman filter papers No. 1 and No. 42 to remove the sediment. The samples were then spiked by adding suitable concentrations of standard Hg2+ to each of the pretreated water samples.

Whitening cream samples were prepared in wet digestion, as previously described in the literature [20]. Briefly, 2 g of whitening cream sample was digested in 10 mL of conc. HNO3 under heating at 80 °C, with magnetic stirring until the solution was clear. A 20 mL aliquot of ultrapure water was then added to the sample solution, which was further heated and stirred for 15 min. The solution was then left to cool, followed by filtration with Whatman filter paper No. 42. The filtrate was transferred to a 100 mL volumetric flask and then adjusted with ultrapure water for quantification of Hg2+ in the samples utilizing N-CDs as fluorescent sensors.

Three types of rice samples and four types of grain samples were obtained from a local supermarket and used for the analysis of thiamine. The rice and grain samples were prepared following a previous report [21]. The samples were dried in an oven at 60 °C for 5 min to remove the moisture, before grinding to a fine powder using a mortar and pestle. A powdered sample (1 g) was placed in a 50 mL beaker and 25 mL of warm water (40 °C) was added. The solution was then stirred with a magnetic stirrer for 30 min before filtering using Whatman filter paper No. 42. The filtrate was used for thiamine detection using N-CDs as fluorescent probes.

Amounts of thiamine in the rice and grain samples were also determined by the thiochrome method for comparison. The thiochrome method is one of the standard methods for analysis of thiamine in samples. Thiamine can be transformed into fluorescent thiochrome by oxidation with potassium hexacyanoferrate(III) under aqueous alkaline condition [22]. In a typical experiment, 3 mL of 0.2 M NaH2PO4–Na2HPO4 buffer at pH 12.2, 3 mL of 10 ppm K3FeCN6, and a known volume of standard solution of thiamine were mixed in a 10 mL volumetric flask and the final volume was adjusted to 10 mL with ultrapure water. The fluorescence intensity at 436 nm of the generated thiochrome was immediately measured using an excitation wavelength of 369 nm. The above procedure was repeated for each standard solution of thiamine.

The N-CDs were prepared via a one-step microwave pyrolysis method, with d-fructose and l-lysine as the carbon source and nitrogen source, respectively. To optimize the synthesized process, the microwave power at 700 W was kept constant with reaction time ranging from 3 to 20 min. Increase in the reaction time resulted in the solution color changing from pale yellow to dark brown, concomitant with the increment of fluorescence intensity at 440 nm, under an excitation wavelength of 360 nm, as shown in Fig. S1. When the reaction time exceeded 10 min, a precipitate formed in the N-CD solution because of the agglomeration of large particles. Therefore, a reaction time of 10 min was chosen for N-CD preparation.

TEM was used to investigate the morphology and size distribution of the prepared N-CDs. As shown in Fig. 1 (a), the N-CD particles were dispersed and spherically shaped without aggregation. Each particle size of the N-CDs was measured using ImageJ. Size distribution of the N-CDs in Fig. 1(b) ranged from 1.52 to 7.58 nm with an average of 5.49 ± 1.90 nm, determined by descriptive statistics (N = 120). The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image in Fig. 1(c) revealed the crystalline nature of the graphitic carbon structure, presenting as adjacent lattice fringes with interplanar spacing of 0.22 nm. The XRD diffraction pattern exhibited a broad peak at around 2θ = 18.0° (Fig. S2(a)), describing the plane of the graphitic structure. The Raman spectrum (Fig. S2(b)) displayed the characteristic peaks of the graphitic structure, the disordered D band at 1330 cm−1, and the crystalline G band at 1579 cm−1 related to the presence of sp3 defect sites and in-plane vibration of sp2-hybridized graphitic carbon, respectively [23]. Another D* band at 1204 cm−1 was attributed to sp2-sp3 bonds in the disordered graphitic lattice, while the peak at 1453 cm−1 was assigned to the A band of sp3-hybridized amorphous carbon [24]. Results from various techniques suggested that the synthesized N-CDs were intermediate between the crystalline phase in the graphitic carbon structure and amorphous carbon.

The surface composition and elemental analysis of the N-CDs were further characterized by XPS and FTIR. Fig. 1(d) shows the XPS survey spectrum of three peaks at 288.2, 400.0, and 532.9 eV corresponding to characteristic peaks of C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s, respectively. The high-resolution spectrum of C 1s (Fig. 1(e)) consisted of three peaks at 284.1, 285.4, and 286.6 eV which were assigned to CC/C–C, C–N, and C–O, respectively [25]. The spectrum of O 1s depicted in Fig. 1(f) contained two peaks at 531.5 eV of CO and 532.9 eV of C–O–C/C–OH [26]. For the N 1s spectrum in Fig. 1(g), two peaks at 400.1 and 401.6 eV were related to the pyridinic N (C–N–C) and N–H [27]. The relative content of elements from the XPS full survey spectrum was 63.18% O, 35.20% C, and 1.62% N. From the FTIR spectrum of N-CDs (Fig. S2(c)), a broad band around 3278 cm−1 was assigned to N–H and O–H stretching vibrations, while a small peak at 2939 cm−1 was attributed to C–H stretching vibration. The other bands at 1340 and 1645 cm−1 were related to C–O and CO bond stretching, respectively with C–N bond stretching located at 1060 cm−1. These results confirmed that the carbon-based structure was successfully doped with nitrogen atoms and the obtained N-doped carbon dots had more oxygen and nitrogen containing surface groups, resulting in excellent dispersion and water solubility.

Optical properties of the N-CDs were studied by UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy. The N-CD solution was pale light yellow under sunlight and gave blue fluorescence under UV light irradiation, as shown in Fig. 2 (a). The UV–vis absorption spectrum presented two narrow peaks at 250 nm and 340 nm, which were attributed to the π–π* transition of CC and the n–π* transition of CO, respectively [28,29]. The solution of N-CDs displayed a strong emission peak at 440 nm under an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. By comparison, for fluorescence spectra of CDs prepared with and without l-lysine doping, as shown in Fig. S3, maximum emission wavelengths of undoped CDs and nitrogen-doped CDs were 452 and 440 nm, respectively. The blue shift of the emission band of nitrogen-doped CDs (N-CDs) compared to undoped CDs implied that the size of N-CDs decreased when doping with l-lysine due to the increasing bandgap between the valence and conduction band [30], while the fluorescence intensity of nitrogen-doped CDs was also higher than undoped CDs. Results revealed that nitrogen atom doping reduced particle size and improved the fluorescence signal of the carbon dots. The fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs were also studied with varying excitation wavelengths ranging from 300 to 400 nm at intervals of 10 nm (Fig. 2(b)). The excitation wavelengths gradually increased from 300 to 360 nm result in increasing fluorescence intensity at the maximum emission peak of about 440 nm. When the excitation wavelengths changed from 360 to 400 nm, the maximum emission wavelengths of N-CDs shifted to longer wavelengths from 440 to 465 nm with concomitant a decrease in fluorescence intensity. The emission wavelength and intensity were dependent on the excitation wavelength due to the distribution of various particle sizes and emissive sites on each passivated carbon dot [31]. In addition, Fig. 2(c) shows the 2D color-converted photoluminescence contour map. The emission band was intense at about 430–470 nm when excited in the range of 350–370 nm. The optimal excitation wavelength of N-CDs was 360 nm, producing the highest fluorescence intensity at 440 nm and was used for further experiments. The shoulder peak at 510 nm was due to an increase in conjugated aromatic π structures which enhanced the rigidity and resulted in the red shift of the absorption and emission wavelengths [31]. The fluorescence intensity of the synthesized N-CD solution was stable for at least 1 month without precipitation for sensing applications.

The UV–vis absorption peaks at 250 nm and 340 nm of the N-CDs increased without optical shift with increasing concentration of Hg2+, as shown in Fig. S4. The broad absorption band at 430 nm, suggested a complex formation between N-CDs and Hg2+. As shown in Fig. 3 (a), the N-CDs exhibited high fluorescence intensity at 440 nm and fluorescence was significantly quenched by addition of Hg2+. This phenomenon was attributed to Hg2+ coordinating with carboxyl, hydroxyl, and amino groups on the N-CD surfaces to form an N-CDs/Hg2+ complex, followed by electron transfer between the N-CDs and Hg2+ [32]. The fluorescence quenching mechanism was static and/or dynamic. The fluorescence lifetime of a fluorophore is unaffected by static quenching, whereas dynamic quenching leads to a change in the fluorophore's fluorescence lifetime [33]. Therefore, fluorescence decay curves of the N-CDs were examined both in the absence and presence of Hg2+. As shown in Fig. 3(b), the fluorescence decay data (emission at 440 nm) could be well fitted to a single-exponential function, implying the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs originated from a single emission state. The fluorescence lifetimes of N-CDs and N-CDs/Hg2+ were 2.16 and 1.28 ns, respectively, while decrease in the fluorescence emission lifetime of N-CDs signified that the complexation of N-CDs by Hg2+ influenced its electronic structure as a result of the dynamic quenching process.

To investigate the fluorescence quenching of N-CDs by Hg2+, causing electron transfer mechanism, the energy levels of the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of N-CDs were estimated with the following empirical formulas [34,35]:
(1)
E LOMO = − e(E Red + 4.4)
(2)
E HOMO = E LOMO − E g
where E LUMO and E HOMO are the energy of the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital and the highest occupied molecular orbital, respectively. E Red is the reduction potential of N-CDs. The E Red was determined to be −0.50 eV by cyclic voltammetry (Fig. S5(a)) and then obtained the E LOMO was −3.90 eV. The combination of energy levels between E LOMO and the optical band gap (Eg) was E HOMO as −7.82 eV, while the E g was estimated to be 3.92 eV from the absorption edge in the characteristic absorption spectrum of N-CDs. The electrode potential of Hg2+ is −5.25 eV [36], which was between E LUMO and E HOMO of N-CDs. When the N-CDs formed complexation with Hg2+, electrons can be transferred from LUMO of N-CDs to the d orbital of Hg2+ (Fig. S5(b)), leading to fluorescence quenching.

The fluorescence quenching capability of N-CDs by Hg2+ was explained by the Stern-Volmer equation as
(3)
F
0 /F = 1 + Ksv[Q]
where F 0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of N-CDs in the absence and presence of Hg2+, respectively, K sv is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, and [Q] is the concentration of quencher (Hg2+). The Stern-Volmer plot of F
0 /F against Hg2+ concentration showed a good linear response with a correlation coefficient of 0.9952 in the range 1–10 μM, as shown in Fig. 3(c) and (d). The value of K sv from the linear response was 2.115 × 105 M−1, and related to an equilibrium constant for the binding of Hg2+ on the N-CD surfaces following the quenching mechanism.

The fluorescence intensity at 440 nm was restored by the addition of thiamine in N-CDs/Hg2+ solution, as shown in Fig. 3(a). Thiamine with a thiazole ring interacted with Hg2+ through the Hg–S bond due to the strong binding ability between soft acid and soft base. The mechanism was verified by zeta potential measurement. Zeta potential of the N-CD solution was −5.98 ± 0.33 mV, suggesting a negative charge on the surface of the particles. When Hg2+ was added to the N-CD solution, the zeta potential increased to 6.36 ± 0.33 mV, suggesting that the surface charge of the particles changed from negative to positive because of electrostatic interaction between the negatively charged N-CDs and Hg2+. In the presence of 2 μM thiamine in the N-CDs/Hg2+ system, the zeta potential was negative at −5.03 ± 0.38 mV and close to the zeta potential of free N-CDs. DLS was used to determine the size distribution of particles in solution. Particle size of the N-CDs was estimated at 8.01 ± 0.33 nm, which was consistent with the TEM result. Size distribution of the particles increased to 173.33 ± 0.33 nm after addition of Hg2+ in the N-CD solution. This significant increment in size distribution implied that Hg2+ coordinated with the surface of N-CDs and induced the formation of aggregation. Addition of thiamine in the N-CDs/Hg2+ solution gave particle diameters as 50.27 ± 0.33 nm, and smaller than N-CDs/Hg2+. This indicated that the sulfur-containing thiazole ring of thiamine was an effectively stronger soft base, which easily removed Hg2+ from the surface of N-CDs, resulting in the fluorescence intensity turning on at 440 nm. Therefore, the “turn off” and “turn on” mechanisms based on N-CDs can be applied to determine Hg2+ and thiamine concentrations.

Selectivity is one of the most important factors of a sensor. Oxygen and nitrogen containing functional groups on the surface of CDs can bind with cations, while CDs have also been used as fluorescence probes for the determination of cations [7]. A solution of each metal cation (Na+, K+, Ag+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Hg2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Al3+, Cr3+, and Fe3+) added to N-CD solution with a final concentration of 6 μM was used to evaluate the selectivity of synthesized N-CDs. As shown in Fig. 4 (a) and (b), fluorescence intensity at 440 nm decreased in the presence of Hg2+, while other metal cations had a negligible effect on fluorescence intensity, even at 10 times the concentration (Fig. S6(a)). Some vitamins such as thiamine, riboflavin, niacin, folic acid, cobalamin, and ascorbic acid had no significant effect on the fluorescence intensity (Fig. S6(c)). Thus, the fluorescence quenching effect of Hg2+ suggested that synthesized N-CDs were highly selective toward Hg2+ over other metal cations, with potential capability as fluorescence probes for the detection of Hg2+.

The fluorescence responses of N-CDs to pH values were investigated in acetic-acetate buffer. The functional groups on the surface of N-CDs were both protonated and deprotonated, resulting in diverse fluorescence responses. Fig. S7(a) shows the fluorescence changes of N-CDs in the absence and presence of 6 μM Hg2+ in acidic, neutral, and basic conditions. The fluorescence quenching efficiency by Hg2+ was low in the pH range 3–5 because carboxyl and amine groups on the N-CD surface were protonated, leading to reduced interaction between N-CDs and Hg2+. Deprotonation of carboxyl groups on the surface of N-CDs occurred at higher pH, resulting in a negatively charged surface, and fluorescence quenching efficiency increased in the presence of Hg2+, as shown in Fig. S7(d). The basic conditions had more hydroxide ions that interfered with Hg2+ detection due to the formation of insoluble mercuric hydroxide [37]. Therefore, pH 6 was selected as the optimal acetic-acetate buffer solution for the next experiments.

The concentration of N-CDs ranging from 0.2 to 2.0 mM in acetic-acetate buffer pH 6 was studied by fixing the Hg2+ concentration. Fig. S7(b) shows the fluorescence emission of various concentrations of N-CDs in the absence and presence of 6 μM Hg2+. When the N-CD concentration exceeded 0.8 mM, the fluorescence emission after addition of 6 μM Hg2+ was greatly quenched. The degree of fluorescence quenching was high and almost unchanged, as shown in Fig. S7(e). Therefore, an N-CD concentration of 0.8 mM was used in further Hg2+ sensing experiments.

The effect of reaction time on the interaction between N-CDs and Hg2+ was also observed in acetic-acetate buffer solution pH 6. In the presence of 6 μM Hg2+, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs was measured at intervals of 3–30 min, as shown in Fig. S7(c). Fluorescence quenching reached stability at 10 min (Fig. S7(f)) because interaction between N-CDs and Hg2+ was complete. Therefore, 10 min was selected as the optimal reaction time for Hg2+ determination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of N-CDs for Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para:
Interaction between N-CDs and Hg2+ resulted in effective fluorescence quenching. To investigate the sensitivity of N-CDs, different concentrations of Hg2+ from 0 to 20 μM were added to N-CD solutions, and the fluorescence spectra were recorded. As shown in Fig. 3(c)–a significant decrease of fluorescence intensity was dependent on an increase in Hg2+ concentration, with no optical shift of fluorescence emission at 440 nm. When the Hg2+ concentration exceeded 10 μM, fluorescence quenching remained constant, with the degree of fluorescence quenching (F
0
–F/F
0 ) of N-CDs proportional to the increase in Hg2+ concentration, as shown in Fig. 5 . Excellent linearity was shown in Hg2+ concentration ranging from 1 to 10 μM, with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9963. The limit of detection (LOD) based on 3Sb was 0.40 μM, while the limit of quantification (LOQ) based on 10Sb was 1.34 μM for Hg2+. Table S1 shows the performance of our proposed probe compared to previously reported carbon-based nanomaterials for an Hg2+ sensor. The limit of detection and a wide linear range for the proposed probe were similar to previous probes but our proposed probe had a short reaction time for preparation, using a microwave-assisted procedure with a small amount of nitrogen doping.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Hg2+ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Practical applications of synthesized N-CDs for the detection of Hg2+ in real water samples (drinking, pond, and tap water) and different whitening cream brands were assessed. As shown in Table 1 , Hg2+ concentration was not detected in water samples but it was detected in whitening cream samples at between 0.431 and 0.656 μM. Recoveries of the spiked samples ranged from 95.38 to 99.17% in water and from 91.14 to 101.95% in whitening cream, with %RSDs less than 3.39% indicating good precision of N-CDs as fluorescent probes to determine Hg2+. The standard ICP technique was used to analyze Hg2+ in the sample solutions to confirm the accuracy of the N-CD fluorescent probe. Results of Hg2+ values in Table 1 showed strong agreement between the ICP-OES method and our proposed method. Based on the analysis of water and cosmetic samples, our proposed probe showed high potential for analysis of Hg2+ with good accuracy and precision.

The N-CDs/Hg2+ system, consisting of 0.8 mM N-CDs and 10 μM Hg2+ in acetic-acetate buffer pH 6, was incubated for 10 min at room temperature. Thiamine was then added to the N-CDs/Hg2+ system. The mixture solution was further incubated at various times and the fluorescence spectrum was recorded. The quenched fluorescence emission gradually recovered and became stable within 5 min (Figs. S8(a) and (b)). To observe the recovered fluorescence of the N-CDs/Hg2+ system, some typical vitamins, cations, and anions including riboflavin, niacin, folic acid, cobalamin, ascorbic acid, Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cl−, NO3 −, and SO4 2− were added to the mixture of N-CDs and Hg2+. The previously mentioned substances had small effects on the fluorescence recovery of the N-CDs/Hg2+ system, as shown in Fig. 6 (a) and (b). These observations indicated that the N-CDs/Hg2+ system showed high selectivity to thiamine, with potential as a useful application for thiamine detection in real food samples.

As shown in Fig. 6(c), fluorescence was restored when the N-CDs/Hg2+system contained 0.2 μM thiamine, which was the minimum amount for effective removal of Hg2+ from the N-CD surface. This observation indicated that fluorescence change was reversible and the efficiency fluorescence recovery at 440 nm was 33.2%. When thiamine concentration increased, the fluorescence of the N-CDs/Hg2+/thiamine system was restored. A good linear relationship between (F
r
–F)/F and thiamine concentration was shown in the range 0.2–2.0 μM, with a correlation coefficient of 0.9987 (Fig. 6(d)) and LOD estimated at 0.03 μM (or 30 nM) and LOQ 0.13 μM. The recovery of fluorescence emission was 91.3% at thiamine concentration of 2.0 μM, while the thiamine concentration exceeded 2.0 μM result in fluorescence recovery between 91.1 and 96.2%, implying the fluorescence intensity could not be recovered completely compared to free N-CDs. In addition, the reversibility of the sensor was performed by alternative addition of Hg2+ and thiamine in N-CD solution (Fig. 7 ). The efficiency of recovery was more than 87.9% to the initial value after six cycles, confirming a good reversibility of the “on-off-on” fluorescent sensor.

To investigate the applicability of the proposed method in real food samples, the N-CDs/Hg2+ system was utilized to determine thiamine in grain food samples including white rice, brown rice, rice berry, white sesame seed, peanut, sunflower seed, and corn. The thiamine content in grain samples is shown in Table 2 . Known amounts of thiamine were added to the samples to obtain the spiked samples. The recoveries and RSD percentages for thiamine in grain samples ranged from 95.31 to 102.53% and from 0.74 to 3.97%, respectively. Results for thiamine values obtained by our proposed method were consistent with those obtained using the standard method. A comparison of linearity range, LOD and recovery percentages between the proposed method and reported methods for thiamine detection is shown in Table 3 . The LOD of this method was down to nanomolar and better than some previous reports. Therefore, our proposed N-CDs/Hg2+ system was practical and reliable used for thiamine determination in real grain food samples.

A fluorescent sensor based on N-CDs was synthesized following a microwave-assisted method using d-fructose and l-lysine as precursors. The synthesized N-CDs showed strong fluorescence intensity at 440 nm under an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. The N-CDs were highly selective toward Hg2+ by quenching fluorescence (turn off), while the introduction of thiamine in the quenched N-CD/Hg2+ system resulted in recovery of the fluorescence response (turn on). The linear range of Hg2+ was 1–10 μM, with a low limit of detection of 0.40 μM. Thiamine had a linear response in the concentration range 0.2–2.0 μM, with lowest detection limit of 30 nM. The “turn off” and “turn on” fluorescent status of N-CDs was used to determine Hg2+ in environmental water, whitening cream samples, and thiamine in grain food samples. This sensor showed promise for analysis of Hg2+ and thiamine in actual samples. The proposed sensor may be an opportunity to apply in a smartphone-based portable fluorescence device for detection of Hg2+ and thiamine.

Suphakan Thanomsak: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Validation, Writing – original draft. Sutthichat Kerdphon: Methodology, Resources. Anchalee Sirikulkajorn: Resources, Investigation. Thawatchai Tuntulani: Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Wanwisa Janrungroatsakul: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Validation, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was financially supported by the Faculty of Science, Naresuan University (grant number R2565E033). W. J. acknowledges financial and laboratory support from the Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Naresuan University. S. T. was supported by the Science Achievement Scholarship of Thailand (SAST).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.optmat.2024.115336.
